title: $FLOW token
summary: Fixed-supply, no public sale, pre-launch status, and why it exists
body_stripped: $FLOW token $FLOW is the unit of account for everything that moves inside AgentFlow. Clients pay for orders in it, operators get paid in it, the reserve accumulates in it, governance votes in it. Outside of the platform it's a BEP 20 token — inside the platform it's the bookkeeping layer. Supply Fixed supply: 1,000,000,000 $FLOW (one billion, final). Admin cannot mint. The mint authority was destroyed at contract deployment. There is no "emergency governance" switch that can bring it back. No public sale. There is no ICO, no presale window, no whitelist drop. The only way to receive $FLOW is through protocol revenue — you operate, you run the affiliate program, you work through the Learner track, or you hold via liquidity that accrues to long term contributors. That last point matters. A lot of token launches use "fair launch" language and still route half the supply to early insiders. $FLOW doesn't have a supply pipe pointed at anyone specific. The 20% slice from every order is the faucet. Vesting Team and investors: zero unlocks for the first 12 months. No drip, no cliff and trickle — just zero until the 12 month mark, at which point the vesting schedule (published separately) begins. Liquidity reserve is a one way sink. No role — admin, governance, multisig — can withdraw from it. Tokens that enter the liquidity reserve stay in the liquidity reserve. What it's for $FLOW is a single in platform unit that does four jobs: 1. Pays for orders. Clients top up in $FLOW (or in fiat that converts into $FLOW for accounting) and pay operators from that balance. 2. Pays out operators. The 60–65% share lands as $FLOW balance, withdrawable to the operator's BEP 20 address. 3. Caps referral rewards. The 100 level affiliate program denominates payouts in $FLOW out of the shared reserve — no fiat liability, the reserve's $FLOW balance is the ceiling. 4. Governs. Holders vote on protocol parameters (operator tiers, vertical allocations, reserve distribution schedules). Pre launch status $FLOW is currently in pre launch. Contract is written, audited, and staged — but the public deployment date is announced separately and intentionally not pinned to a specific week in marketing copy. The reason: until the contract goes live, balances shown in the platform are virtual ledger entries that will map 1:1 to on chain $FLOW at launch. If you've been earning $FLOW from orders or referrals in the meantime, those balances are reserved for you and claimable at launch. What $FLOW is not Not a security. Not an investment offering. Does not promise returns. Not unlimited. No "governance vote to mint more" escape hatch. The one billion is the one billion. Not universally available. Some jurisdictions restrict participation. If you're in one of them, the UI will surface that during signup.

title: 14-day escrow
summary: How client funds are held, released, and disputed
body_stripped: 14 day escrow When a client pays for an order on AgentFlow, the money does not go straight to the operator. It lands on an escrow contract and sits there for fourteen days. The timer is a warranty window: the client has two full weeks to test the work, ask for revisions, and decide whether the result actually matches the brief. How release works The day after the client accepts the work, the operator starts vesting in small tranches — 2% of the order per day for 14 days . The operator can withdraw each vested slice as it unlocks, which keeps cashflow moving without handing over the full amount at once. On day 15 the remaining 72% releases as a single payout . By that point the warranty window has closed, the client has had every chance to flag issues, and the bulk of the money is free to move. This split is intentional. The daily drip gives operators real money during the warranty period, while the back loaded 72% gives clients a real lever if something breaks late in the cycle — the largest chunk is still on the table until the end. Minimums and priority Minimum order: $10. Below that the protocol overhead eats the economics. $100 and up goes into the priority queue and is eligible for the livestream showcase where operators watch new orders land. The first 150 cases form the early access window. After that, queueing opens up and any verified operator can pick up work. Disputes If the client thinks the work doesn't match the brief, they open a dispute inside the escrow window. Two things happen: 1. An admin audit — AgentFlow staff review the brief, the deliverable, and the message history. 2. A jury of three top operators votes, and a 2 of 3 majority decides the outcome. The jury can rule three ways: Refund the client (operator gets nothing). Pay the operator in full (warranty releases early). Split the funds between client and operator based on how much of the brief was actually delivered. Because the jury is made of operators who have shipped real work, they tend to catch both bad operators and clients trying to scope creep after the fact. The 2 of 3 threshold keeps a single biased juror from swinging the result. Why this shape The warranty exists because agent built work can look correct on day one and break on day three. The 14 day horizon covers the most common failure modes — broken integrations, copy that falls apart under real traffic, landing pages that miss on mobile. By the time the final 72% releases, either the work held up or the dispute already happened.

title: Deal split
summary: How every order divides between reserve, agent owner, and operator
body_stripped: Deal split Every paid order on AgentFlow gets cut into three slices the moment the client accepts the work. There is no separate platform fee — the split itself is the economics. The three slices 20% → shared $FLOW reserve. This replaces the old flat 1% platform fee. Instead of skimming a service charge into a company wallet, a fifth of every deal flows into a public reserve that feeds affiliate rewards, operator stakes, and future token distribution. 15–20% → agent owner royalty. The person who assembled and registered the agent earns on every deal that agent closes. Owners tune the character, wire up the plugins, keep the prompts current — the royalty is what makes that ongoing work worth doing. 60–65% → operator share. The human who took the order and shipped the work. This share also covers model costs — operators pick which LLMs to run, pay inference out of pocket, and keep whatever is left after compute. The 15–20% / 60–65% range moves inside a narrow band depending on the vertical and the tier of the operator, but the 20% into the reserve is fixed. Plus 1% from every deposit There is one more flow that isn't tied to a specific order: 1% of every new deposit into the platform goes into the shared $FLOW reserve the day it lands . A $1,000 deposit means $10 into the reserve before the client has even picked a task. This is why the reserve keeps growing even during quiet weeks — fresh money coming into the platform always feeds it. What the reserve does The reserve isn't just a number in a dashboard. It has jobs: 1. Affiliate rewards. The 100 level referral program pays out of the reserve, not out of the operator's or client's pocket. If you brought in the client, their deal tops up your future claims. 2. Operator stakes. Once $FLOW is live, operators stake from the reserve balance to unlock higher tier orders — bigger stake, bigger jobs. 3. Protocol distribution. Remaining $FLOW from the reserve distributes to long term participants through governance decisions — this is where the "who holds $FLOW" question gets answered. Why no platform fee A flat fee punishes small orders: $10 orders can't afford a 20% middleman. Pulling the economics into the split itself means the smallest deals work at the same margin as the largest ones, and the reserve grows proportionally to real activity, not proportionally to a service charge someone had to justify.
